fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a codec utility library for our Lambda event pipeline. Basically our consumers are drowning in boilerplate every time they try to read a DynamoDB stream record or process a CloudWatch Logs subscription — everyone's hand-rolling the same conversions differently and we keep getting weird silent bugs when someone assumes a number field is already an int or forgets to gunzip the logs payload before parsing it. 

The library should handle the three main pain points we keep hitting: (1) those typed attribute wrapper objects from the document store — you know the ones with the single-key envelope format we discussed in the infra sync — (2) the timestamp fields that sometimes come in as seconds and sometimes as millis depending on which service is sending, and (3) the compressed log batch field in CloudWatch delivery events. 

For the timestamp stuff, refer to how we handled the precision-sensitive round-trip in the billing module — same idea here but we need both directions. Wrong-type access should blow up loudly, not silently coerce. 

Also need a test runner script that can point at different case directories so QA can swap in their own fixtures without touching the harness itself. Output files should be namespaced so parallel runs don't stomp each other. Single stdin-to-stdout adapter for the test interface, core logic fully separated.

trigger_keywords: what order do map fields appear in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Map entries (type M) must be reported with their keys sorted in ascending lexicographic order. For example a map with keys 'Name' and 'Age' outputs the 'Age' entry before the 'Name' entry. This ordering is required in both the full decode output and when a map value appears as an element inside a list or nested map.

trigger_keywords: how to truncate float to int
negative number truncation for integer conversion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When converting a Number attribute to integer and the decimal string contains a fractional part, the value must be parsed as a floating-point number first and then truncated toward zero (not floored). This means -123.9 becomes -123, not -124. The action key for this operation is 'dynamodb_number' and the 'as' field is 'integer'.

trigger_keywords: what does the incompatible type error look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A type-mismatch error must produce exactly three lines: 'error=incompatible_type', then 'requested=<RequestedTypeName>', then 'actual=<ActualTypeName>'. The type names used are the logical domain names: String, Number, Binary, Boolean, Null, StringSet, NumberSet, BinarySet, List, Map — not any host-language class names. The action for triggering this check is 'dynamodb_access' with a mismatched 'as' field.

trigger_keywords: how many decimal places for seconds epoch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When encoding to epoch seconds ('epoch_seconds_encode'), the output line is 'epoch=<value>' where the value retains only the fractional digits needed to represent sub-second precision. For example 476 milliseconds yields 'epoch=1480641523.476' — three decimal places, no trailing zeros beyond what is needed. If the instant has no sub-second component the output should be a plain integer representation.

When encoding to epoch milliseconds ('epoch_millis_encode'), the output line is 'epoch=<value>' where the value is a whole integer count of milliseconds — no decimal point. For example '2016-12-02T01:18:43.476Z' yields 'epoch=1480641523476'.

The compressed log batch payload is nested under the 'awslogs' key in the request object, and then under the 'data' key inside that: request['awslogs']['data']. The action name is 'cloudwatch_logs_parse'. The value is a base64-encoded gzip-compressed JSON document.

The CloudWatch log batch output must follow this exact field order: owner, logGroup, logStream, subscriptionFilters, messageType, events (count), then one line per log entry in the format '[<index>] id=<id> timestamp=<millis> message=<message>'. Multiple subscription filters are reported as a single comma-separated list on the subscriptionFilters line. Index is zero-based.

trigger_keywords: how to run different test fixtures
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness entry point is 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument to point at a directory of JSON case files relative to rcb_tests/ (default is 'public_test_cases'). Output files are written to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/' and named '<filename-stem>@<zero-padded-3-digit-case-index>.txt', containing only the raw stdout from the program under test — no PASS/FAIL annotations.

The NULL attribute type (wire key 'NULL') reports 'type=Null' and then a null indicator line 'value=NULL' rather than a blank or absent value line. No element count is reported since NULL is a scalar sentinel, not a collection.

trigger_keywords: how are L type elements reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A List attribute (wire key 'L') reports 'type=List', then 'count=<n>', then one line per element. Each element line reports the element's own resolved type and its scalar value on the same line, formatted as 'type=<Type> value=<value>'. Elements are reported in their original order. An empty list reports count=0 with no element lines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'dynamodb_decode', 'dynamodb_number', 'dynamodb_access', 'epoch_seconds_decode', 'epoch_seconds_encode', 'epoch_millis_decode', 'epoch_millis_encode', 'cloudwatch_logs_parse'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the typed attribute wrapper objects from the document store — you know the ones with the single-key envelope format we discussed in the infra sync
context_pointers.actual_meaning: DynamoDB attribute value wire format: a JSON object with exactly one key that names the logical type (S, N, BOOL, B, NULL, SS, NS, BS, L, M) and whose value is the encoded payload, as specified in Feature 1 of start.md and tested in feature1_1_decode_attribute_value.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to how we handled the precision-sensitive round-trip in the billing module — same idea here but we need both directions
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Epoch timestamp codec supporting both decode (epoch number → UTC calendar components) and encode (ISO-8601 UTC string → epoch number) directions, at both seconds resolution (fractional) and milliseconds resolution (whole integer), as specified in Feature 2 of start.md and tested in feature2_1_seconds_epoch.json and feature2_2_milliseconds_epoch.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect from the original discussion. Could you check the PRD document or the test case JSON files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact contract? The test cases are the authoritative source for expected output formatting.

feature1_1_decode_attribute_value.json.description: Decode a single attribute value given in the wire format used by a NoSQL document store, where every value is a JSON object with exactly one key naming its type and the matching encoded payload. The decoder reports the resolved logical type and the value. Scalars report their value directly; binary payloads are reported as their standard base64 text; collection types report their element count followed by one line per element (sets list their members, a heterogeneous list reports each element's own type and scalar value, and a map reports each entry keyed by name with its element type and scalar value, entries ordered by key); the null type reports a null indicator.

cases.expected_output:
type=Map
count=2
Age: type=Number value=35
Name: type=String value=Joe

feature1_2_number_conversion.json.description: Convert a numeric attribute value (which arrives on the wire as a decimal string) into a native numeric form on request. When an integer is requested, the value is parsed as a signed integer; if the string holds a fractional decimal, it is first parsed as a floating-point number and then truncated toward zero to an integer. When a floating-point value is requested, the string is parsed as a real number. The output reports the requested numeric form and the resulting value.

feature1_3_type_mismatch_error.json.description: Request a typed accessor on an attribute value whose actual decoded type differs from the requested one. Accessing a value as a type it does not hold is a contract violation and must be rejected rather than silently coerced. The output is a normalized error contract that names the error category together with the requested type and the actual type of the value, so callers can diagnose the mismatch without depending on any host-language runtime details.

feature2_1_seconds_epoch.json.description: Serialize and deserialize an instant of time expressed as a UNIX epoch measured in seconds, where fractional seconds are permitted. Decoding takes the epoch-seconds number (possibly fractional) and yields the corresponding calendar instant in UTC, reported as its individual components down to the millisecond. Encoding takes a calendar instant given as an ISO-8601 UTC timestamp and yields the epoch-seconds number, keeping the fractional part needed to represent sub-second precision.

cases.expected_output:
year=2016
month=12
day=2
hour=1
minute=18
second=43
millisecond=476

feature2_2_milliseconds_epoch.json.description: Serialize and deserialize an instant of time expressed as a UNIX epoch measured in whole milliseconds. Decoding takes the epoch-milliseconds integer and yields the corresponding calendar instant in UTC, reported as its individual components down to the millisecond. Encoding takes a calendar instant given as an ISO-8601 UTC timestamp and yields the epoch-milliseconds integer.

cases.expected_output:
year=2016
month=12
day=2
hour=1
minute=18
second=43
millisecond=476

feature3_cloudwatch_logs.json.description: Decode a managed-log delivery payload. The event carries a single opaque field holding the log batch as text that has been gzip-compressed and then base64-encoded. Decoding base64-decodes the field, decompresses it, and parses the embedded JSON document to recover the batch metadata (the account owner, the log group and stream names, the configured subscription filters, and the message type) together with the ordered list of log entries, each with its identifier, millisecond timestamp, and raw message text. The output reports the metadata fields, the number of entries, and one line per entry.

cases.expected_output:
owner=123456789123
logGroup=testLogGroup
logStream=testLogStream
subscriptionFilters=testFilter
messageType=DATA_MESSAGE
events=2
[0] id=eventId1 timestamp=1440442987000 message=[ERROR] First test message
[1] id=eventId2 timestamp=1440442987001 message=[ERROR] Second test message
